events.text: The patient is a 66-year-old male named Raymond Kowalski. He is a retired pipe fitter with a 30 pack-year smoking history (quit 15 years prior), type 2 diabetes mellitus managed with metformin, and hypertension. He has no family history of pancreatic cancer. Eastern Cooperative Oncology Group (ECOG) performance status is 1.

At age 66 years, the patient had a diagnosis of Stage III (AJCC 8th edition, cT3N1M0) PANCREATIC DUCTAL ADENOCARCINOMA (ICD-O histology code 8500/3), primary site: HEAD OF PANCREAS (ICD-O site code C25.0). The tumor measured 3.8 cm in greatest dimension on cross-sectional imaging. The tumor was classified as BORDERLINE RESECTABLE per NCCN/AHPBA criteria, with abutment of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) over less than 180 degrees and short-segment abutment/encasement of the superior mesenteric vein-portal vein confluence (SMV). There was no distant metastatic disease. Regional lymph nodes were radiographically suspicious. Serum CA 19-9 was markedly elevated at 1200 U/mL at diagnosis. CEA was mildly elevated at 4.8 ng/mL. Total bilirubin was 2.1 mg/dL. Confirmation: POSITIVE HISTOLOGY via EUS-guided FNA. Summary stage: REGIONAL. Relevant site-specific data: Exocrine pancreas primary; no prior pancreatic surgery; biliary stenting performed.

At age 66 years, the patient had a pathology result of type CYTOPATHOLOGY/EUS-FNA. Specimen source: pancreatic head mass, endoscopic ultrasound-guided fine needle aspiration. Diagnosis: ADENOCARCINOMA, consistent with pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. Cytomorphology showed malignant glandular cells arranged in clusters with nuclear pleomorphism, irregular nuclear membranes, prominent nucleoli, and anisonucleosis. Background showed desmoplastic stromal fragments. IHC: CK7 positive, CK20 negative, CDX2 negative, MUC1 positive, MUC2 negative. Conclusion: MALIGNANT — ductal adenocarcinoma of the pancreas.

At age 66 years, the patient had a CT ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH PANCREATIC PROTOCOL (triphasic), which showed cancer. Findings: 3.8 cm ill-defined, hypovascular mass in the head of the pancreas causing upstream pancreatic ductal dilation (6 mm) and mild common bile duct dilation. Tumor abuts the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) over approximately 120-150 degrees of circumference. Short-segment involvement/abutment of the SMV-portal vein confluence without occlusion. Two periportal lymph nodes measuring up to 1.2 cm, suspicious for nodal involvement. No hepatic metastases. No peritoneal implants. No ascites. Impression: Borderline resectable pancreatic head adenocarcinoma, cT3N1M0, Stage III. Vascular involvement: SMA abutment < 180 degrees; SMV/PV short-segment abutment. Metastatic sites: None identified.

At age 66 years, the patient had a PET/CT WHOLE BODY (FDG), which showed cancer. Findings: Hypermetabolic mass in the pancreatic head (SUVmax 8.4) consistent with primary malignancy. Two mildly FDG-avid periportal lymph nodes (SUVmax 3.1 and 2.8), suspicious for metastatic involvement. No hepatic lesions with pathologic FDG uptake. No distant metastatic disease. No peritoneal carcinomatosis. Impression: Locally advanced pancreatic head adenocarcinoma without evidence of distant metastases. Findings support borderline resectable classification.

At age 66 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed for pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma based on a specimen obtained from a pancreatic head FNA (EUS-guided). Tumor mutational burden: 3.2 mutations/Mb (LOW). Microsatellite status: MICROSATELLITE STABLE (MSS). Actionable alterations: KRAS p.G12V mutation (VAF 42%, pathogenic, oncogenic driver — no approved targeted therapy available; not eligible for KRAS G12C inhibitors); TP53 p.R248W mutation (VAF 38%, pathogenic, loss-of-function); SMAD4 deletion (homozygous loss, pathogenic — associated with systemic metastatic propensity). CDKN2A homozygous deletion. Co-mutations and other findings: STK11 wild-type; BRCA1 wild-type; BRCA2 wild-type (no homologous recombination deficiency detected); ATM wild-type; PALB2 wild-type; MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2 wild-type (mismatch repair proficient); ARID1A wild-type; RNF43 wild-type. Copy number alterations: MYC gain (low-level amplification); ERBB2 copy number 2.8 (borderline, not amplified). Fusions: no clinically relevant fusions detected (ALK, RET, NTRK1/2/3, ROS1, FGFR2 all negative). NTRK fusion negative. Germline recommendation: BRCA1/2 and ATM germline testing recommended per NCCN guidelines given pancreatic adenocarcinoma diagnosis. PD-L1 expression: CPS 1 (low). Conclusion: KRAS G12V-driven pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, MSS, HRD-negative, no actionable targeted therapy identified beyond standard chemotherapy.

At age 66 years, the patient had a multidisciplinary tumor board (hepatopancreatobiliary team) assessment. Cancer present. Discussion: Patient with newly diagnosed borderline resectable pancreatic head adenocarcinoma, cT3N1M0, CA 19-9 1200 U/mL, KRAS G12V/TP53/SMAD4-mutated, MSS, HRD-negative. Biliary decompression performed via ERCP with plastic biliary stent placed given bilirubin elevation of 2.1. Consensus recommendation: Neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX chemotherapy for 8 cycles (approximately 4 months) with restaging imaging after cycle 4 and cycle 8, followed by reassessment for resectability. Plan for germline BRCA/ATM testing (pending). ECOG PS 1. Patient agreeable to neoadjuvant strategy. Baseline CA 19-9: 1200 U/mL.

At age 66 years, the patient received neoadjuvant-intent FOLFIRINOX (oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2 IV, irinotecan 180 mg/m2 IV, leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV, 5-fluorouracil 400 mg/m2 IV bolus then 2400 mg/m2 IV over 46 hours) every 2 weeks. Cycle 1 of 8 initiated. Prophylactic pegfilgrastim administered on day 2. Ondansetron and dexamethasone used as antiemetic prophylaxis. Atropine/loperamide provided for irinotecan-related diarrhea prophylaxis. Dose reductions planned per protocol if grade 3-4 toxicity. Metformin held on chemotherapy days per institutional protocol.

At age 66 years (during FOLFIRINOX cycles 1-2), the patient experienced grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy (oxaliplatin-induced), grade 2 fatigue, grade 1-2 nausea, and grade 1 alopecia. Additionally, grade 2 diarrhea was noted after cycle 2, managed with loperamide titration and dietary modification. No dose reductions required at this stage. No febrile neutropenia. ANC nadir approximately 1.2 x 10^9/L (grade 2 neutropenia, asymptomatic). Weight loss of 3 kg noted over the initial 4 weeks.

At age 66 years (after FOLFIRINOX cycle 4, approximately 2 months into neoadjuvant therapy), the patient had a CT ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH PANCREATIC PROTOCOL (restaging), which showed cancer with response to therapy. Findings: Pancreatic head mass now measures 3.1 cm (previously 3.8 cm), representing interval reduction in size. Decreased perilesional stranding. SMA abutment now appears to involve less than 90 degrees of circumference (improved from 120-150 degrees). SMV/PV abutment stable, without new venous occlusion. Suspicious periportal lymph nodes appear slightly decreased in size (largest now 0.8 cm). No new hepatic lesions. No peritoneal disease. Impression: Partial response by modified RECIST criteria. Continued improvement in vascular abutment. CA 19-9 at time of scan: 380 U/mL (down from 1200). Cancer status: Present, responding.

At age 66 years, the patient had an oncologist office assessment after FOLFIRINOX cycle 4. Cancer present. Response to therapy noted. CA 19-9 trending down to 380 U/mL from baseline 1200 U/mL. Imaging shows partial response with improved vascular involvement. Patient tolerating therapy reasonably well with grade 2 neuropathy and managed GI toxicity. Decision: Continue FOLFIRINOX for 4 additional cycles (total 8 cycles planned). Dose of oxaliplatin reduced by 20% (to 68 mg/m2) due to cumulative peripheral neuropathy. ECOG PS 1 maintained. Nutritional status monitored; pancreatic enzyme replacement initiated for exocrine insufficiency.

At age 66 years (during FOLFIRINOX cycles 5-8), the patient experienced progression of peripheral sensory neuropathy to grade 2-3 (cumulative oxaliplatin toxicity), characterized by numbness and tingling in bilateral hands and feet, mild impairment of fine motor skills. Grade 2 fatigue persisted. Cycle 7 complicated by grade 3 diarrhea requiring hospitalization for 2 days for IV hydration and electrolyte repletion; subsequent dose reduction of irinotecan to 135 mg/m2 (75% dose). One episode of grade 2 oral mucositis. Total weight loss from baseline: 7 kg. Nutritional support optimized with oral supplements and continued pancreatic enzyme replacement.

At age 66 years (after FOLFIRINOX cycle 8, approximately 4 months into neoadjuvant therapy), the patient had a CT ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH PANCREATIC PROTOCOL (restaging), which showed cancer with ongoing response to therapy. Findings: Pancreatic head mass now measures 2.4 cm (previously 3.1 cm at mid-treatment, 3.8 cm at baseline). SMA abutment reduced to less than 90 degrees of circumference; SMA fat plane partially restored. SMV/PV interface appears improved; short-segment contact without definite encasement. Periportal lymph nodes now appear within normal size limits (largest 0.6 cm, previously 1.2 cm). No new lesions. No hepatic metastases. No peritoneal disease. Impression: Continued partial response. Improved vascular relationships suggesting potential downstaging. Tumor may now be resectable. CA 19-9: 85 U/mL (down from 1200 at baseline). Cancer status: Present, responding.

At age 66 years, the patient had a multidisciplinary tumor board reassessment after completing 8 cycles of FOLFIRINOX. Cancer present. Significant response: CA 19-9 decreased from 1200 to 85 U/mL; tumor decreased from 3.8 to 2.4 cm; improved vascular involvement. Disease trajectory: Responding. Tumor board discussion: Patient has demonstrated excellent biochemical and radiographic response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy. Vascular abutment of SMA appears improved; surgical team believes resection is now feasible with potential for R0 resection, though vascular reconstruction of SMV may still be required intraoperatively. Plan: Proceed with chemoradiation (long-course radiation with concurrent capecitabine) as consolidation prior to surgery, then restaging and surgical planning for pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure). Neuropathy now at grade 2; oxaliplatin-based therapy complete. Overall performance status ECOG 1.

At age 66 years, the patient received chemoradiation with concurrent capecitabine as consolidative neoadjuvant therapy following FOLFIRINOX. Radiation therapy: Intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) to the pancreatic head and regional lymphatics. Total dose: 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions (1.8 Gy/fraction) delivered over 5.5 weeks. Concurrent radiosensitizing chemotherapy: capecitabine 825 mg/m2 orally twice daily on radiation days. Planning target volume included gross tumor volume plus regional nodes (celiac axis, SMA, portal/SMV nodal regions). Organs at risk: duodenum, stomach, small bowel, spinal cord, kidneys, liver constrained per AAPM TG-101 guidelines. Treatment completed without interruption.

At age 66 years (during chemoradiation), the patient experienced grade 2 radiation-related nausea and vomiting managed with scheduled ondansetron and prochlorperazine. Grade 2 fatigue. Grade 1-2 hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia) attributed to capecitabine, managed with emollient creams and dose reduction of capecitabine to 750 mg/m2 BID. Grade 1 radiation dermatitis over the epigastric treatment field. Mildly elevated liver enzymes (ALT 68 U/L, grade 1) that normalized spontaneously. Pre-existing peripheral neuropathy (grade 2) remained stable. Weight loss stabilized; total from baseline: 8 kg. Patient completed full course of chemoradiation without treatment breaks.

At age 66 years (approximately 6 weeks after completion of chemoradiation, pre-surgical restaging), the patient had a CT ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH PANCREATIC PROTOCOL, which showed cancer. Findings: Pancreatic head mass now measures 2.1 cm (previously 2.4 cm after FOLFIRINOX). Hypodense area with central low-attenuation component suggesting treatment effect/necrosis. SMA fat plane appears largely restored; abutment estimated at less than 60 degrees if any residual contact. SMV/PV confluence appears patent without definite tumor encasement. Regional lymph nodes within normal size. No hepatic metastases. No peritoneal implants. CA 19-9: 62 U/mL. Impression: Continued response to multimodality neoadjuvant therapy. Tumor appears potentially resectable with likely R0 margins. Recommend surgical consultation for Whipple procedure planning. Cancer status: Present, ongoing partial response.

At age 66 years, the patient had a hepatopancreatobiliary surgical consultation. Cancer present. Preoperative assessment: Patient has completed neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX x 8 cycles followed by chemoradiation with capecitabine. CA 19-9 has declined from 1200 to 62 U/mL. Imaging demonstrates radiographic downstaging with improved vascular anatomy. Patient ECOG PS 1, adequate nutritional status with recent 8 kg weight loss from baseline but now stabilized. Preoperative cardiac clearance obtained (stress test negative for ischemia). Pulmonary function adequate. Anesthesia pre-assessment completed. Decision: Proceed with pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure). Intraoperative vascular reconstruction of SMV may be required; vascular surgery on standby. Patient consented for surgery including potential portal vein/SMV resection and reconstruction.

At age 67 years (approximately 7 months after initial diagnosis), the patient underwent PANCREATICODUODENECTOMY (WHIPPLE PROCEDURE) — open approach — for pancreatic head adenocarcinoma following neoadjuvant therapy. Intraoperative findings: Tumor in head of pancreas, 2.2 cm, with dense fibrosis consistent with treatment effect. SMA was dissected free with complete medial dissection; no arterial involvement requiring reconstruction. SMV demonstrated short-segment involvement with desmoplastic adhesion; segmental SMV resection and primary reconstruction (end-to-end anastomosis) performed with vascular surgery assistance. No peritoneal metastases. Liver surface inspection and palpation: no gross metastases. Intraoperative frozen sections of pancreatic neck margin: NEGATIVE. Operative time: 6 hours 45 minutes. Estimated blood loss: 650 mL; 2 units packed red blood cells transfused intraoperatively. Specimens: pancreatic head, duodenum, proximal jejunum, gallbladder, distal common bile duct, regional lymph nodes. Reconstruction: standard pancreaticojejunostomy (duct-to-mucosa), hepaticojejunostomy, gastrojejunostomy. Patient transferred to surgical ICU postoperatively in stable condition.

At age 67 years, the patient had a pathology result of type SURGICAL PATHOLOGY — PANCREATICODUODENECTOMY SPECIMEN. Gross: Pancreatic head specimen 12 x 7 x 5 cm with duodenum 25 cm. Tumor: 2.0 cm residual viable adenocarcinoma in head of pancreas with extensive fibrosis, necrosis, and treatment effect. Histology: MODERATELY DIFFERENTIATED DUCTAL ADENOCARCINOMA, pancreatobiliary type. Treatment response: COLLEGE OF AMERICAN PATHOLOGISTS (CAP) tumor response grade 2 (moderate response; residual cancer with evident treatment effect). Margins: Pancreatic neck margin: NEGATIVE (R0, clearance > 1 mm); Posterior margin: NEGATIVE (R0, 1.5 mm clearance); Anterior margin: NEGATIVE (R0, 2.1 mm clearance); SMV/portal vein groove margin: NEGATIVE (R0, 1.0 mm clearance). Common bile duct margin: NEGATIVE. All surgical margins: R0 RESECTION ACHIEVED. Lymph nodes: 3 of 18 lymph nodes positive for metastatic adenocarcinoma (pN1). Perineural invasion: PRESENT. Lymphovascular invasion: PRESENT. Pathologic stage: ypT2N1M0 — Stage IIB (AJCC 8th edition, post-treatment). IHC: CK7+, CK20-, CDX2-; SMAD4 loss confirmed. KRAS mutation confirmed by IHC surrogate (strong nuclear p53 staining consistent with TP53 gain-of-function mutation).

At age 67 years, the patient had a postoperative surgical assessment at 2 weeks after Whipple procedure. Cancer: R0 resection achieved, ypT2N1M0 (Stage IIB). Postoperative course: Patient was in surgical ICU for 2 days, then transferred to surgical floor. Tolerating clear liquids advancing to soft diet by postoperative day 5. Delayed gastric emptying (grade A per ISGPS criteria) managed conservatively with nasogastric decompression and prokinetic therapy (metoclopramide); resolved by postoperative day 10. Wound healing adequate. Drain amylase checked postoperative day 3: elevated at 1850 U/L (greater than 3x upper limit of normal), concerning for early pancreatic fistula formation. Drain kept in place. Patient discharged home postoperative day 12 with drain in situ. Plan: adjuvant chemotherapy with gemcitabine/capecitabine (APACT regimen) to begin 6-8 weeks postoperatively pending recovery. Oncology follow-up arranged. CA 19-9: 28 U/mL (postoperative).

At age 67 years (postoperative weeks 3-6 following Whipple procedure), the patient developed a CLINICALLY RELEVANT PANCREATIC FISTULA — Grade B per International Study Group on Pancreatic Fistula (ISGPF/ISGPS) criteria. Clinical presentation: persistent drain output with high amylase (drain fluid amylase > 5000 U/L at week 3), low-grade fever (38.1 degrees C), mild epigastric discomfort, and leukocytosis (WBC 13.2 x 10^9/L). CT abdomen demonstrated a peri-anastomotic fluid collection (4.5 cm) with no pneumobilia and no free air, consistent with pancreatic fistula/leak from pancreaticojejunostomy. Management: Patient readmitted for IV antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam), NPO with total parenteral nutrition (TPN) initiated, drain repositioned under CT guidance. Octreotide 100 mcg subcutaneously TID initiated to reduce pancreatic secretion. Fistula output gradually declined over 3 weeks. TPN continued for 3 weeks; parenteral nutrition transitioned to enteral feeds via nasojejunal tube. Drain removed at week 10 postoperatively after output < 10 mL/day with normalized amylase. Full complication management duration: approximately 5-6 weeks. Adjuvant chemotherapy initiation delayed to 10 weeks postoperatively due to complication.

At age 67 years, the patient had an oncologist office assessment at 10 weeks post-Whipple procedure, following resolution of pancreatic fistula. Cancer: R0 resection, no evidence of disease on recent imaging. Disease trajectory: Post-resection. Patient recovered well from Grade B pancreatic fistula; TPN discontinued, oral diet tolerated. Weight 4 kg below pre-complication baseline but trending upward. ECOG PS 1. CA 19-9: 22 U/mL (within normal range, downtrending). Peripheral neuropathy grade 1-2 (residual from oxaliplatin). Plan: Initiate adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine per APACT regimen: gemcitabine 1000 mg/m2 IV on days 1, 8, 15 of each 4-week cycle; capecitabine 1660 mg/m2/day orally in divided doses on days 1-21. Target 6 cycles (24 weeks). Pancreatic enzyme replacement continued (creon). Close monitoring of hepatic function given prior chemoradiation field. Baseline labs: CBC, CMP, CA 19-9 obtained.

At age 67 years, the patient received adjuvant GEMCITABINE/CAPECITABINE (APACT regimen) following Whipple procedure with R0 resection (ypT2N1M0 pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, post-neoadjuvant therapy). Gemcitabine 1000 mg/m2 IV over 30 minutes on days 1, 8, 15 of each 28-day cycle; capecitabine 830 mg/m2 orally twice daily (1660 mg/m2/day total) on days 1-21. Cycle 1 initiated at 10 weeks postoperatively. Full-dose initiation given ECOG PS 1 and adequate organ function (creatinine 1.0 mg/dL, bilirubin 0.8 mg/dL, AST/ALT within normal limits). Prophylactic antiemetics: ondansetron PRN. Duration planned: 6 cycles over 24 weeks.

At age 67 years (during adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine cycles 1-3), the patient experienced grade 2 fatigue, grade 1-2 nausea managed with ondansetron, and grade 1 hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia from capecitabine — managed with emollient cream and dose reduction of capecitabine to 750 mg/m2 BID in cycle 2). Grade 1-2 thrombocytopenia (platelet nadir 88 x 10^9/L in cycle 2) — gemcitabine day 15 held in cycle 2 per protocol. Grade 1 transaminase elevation (ALT 52 U/L). Pre-existing grade 1-2 peripheral neuropathy from prior oxaliplatin remained stable. No febrile neutropenia. No further infectious complications related to prior pancreatic fistula. Residual exocrine insufficiency managed with pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (creon 36,000 units per meal). New-onset or worsened glycemic control noted; insulin therapy (basal-bolus regimen) initiated as metformin held.

At age 67 years (approximately 3 months after Whipple procedure, mid-adjuvant therapy), the patient had a CT ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST (surveillance), which showed no cancer. Findings: Post-Whipple anatomy with expected postsurgical changes. Surgical anastomoses intact. No pancreatic bed recurrence. No hepatic lesions. No peritoneal implants. No new lymphadenopathy. No ascites. Residual mild mesenteric fat stranding in the surgical bed, consistent with expected postoperative changes. Impression: No evidence of recurrent or metastatic disease. Cancer status: Absent. CA 19-9: 18 U/mL (normal).

At age 67 years, the patient had an oncologist office assessment after completing adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine cycle 4 of 6. Cancer: No evidence of disease (NED). Disease trajectory: Neither progressing nor responding — in remission, receiving adjuvant therapy. Patient continues to tolerate adjuvant therapy with manageable toxicity. CA 19-9 now 18 U/mL (normal). CT surveillance shows no recurrence. Peripheral neuropathy remains grade 1-2 (feet > hands); referral to neurology for symptomatic management with duloxetine 30 mg daily initiated. Diabetes management: Endocrinology co-management; basal insulin (glargine) plus mealtime insulin (aspart) titrated. Weight: improved, now 5 kg above post-fistula nadir, still 3 kg below pre-diagnosis baseline. Plan: Complete cycles 5 and 6, then transition to surveillance per NCCN guidelines (CT every 3-4 months for first 2 years, CA 19-9 every 3 months).

At age 67 years, the patient had an oncologist office assessment after completing all 6 cycles of adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine. Cancer: No evidence of disease (NED). Adjuvant chemotherapy completed. Summary: Patient with ypT2N1M0 pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12V, TP53, SMAD4, CDKN2A mutations) treated with: (1) Neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX x 8 cycles, (2) Chemoradiation with capecitabine, (3) R0 Whipple procedure with segmental SMV reconstruction, (4) Adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine x 6 cycles. Total treatment duration from diagnosis to adjuvant completion: approximately 16-17 months. Current ECOG PS 1. CA 19-9: 15 U/mL (normal). Residual grade 1 peripheral neuropathy in bilateral feet. Ongoing pancreatic exocrine insufficiency managed with enzyme replacement. Insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus post-pancreatectomy. Transitioning to surveillance protocol: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis every 3-4 months x 2 years, then every 6 months x 3 years; CA 19-9 every 3 months.

At age 67 years (approximately 6 months post-Whipple, first post-adjuvant surveillance scan), the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST (surveillance), which showed no cancer. Findings: No evidence of locoregional recurrence in the surgical bed. No hepatic lesions. No pulmonary nodules or masses. No new lymphadenopathy. No pleural or peritoneal effusion. Pancreaticojejunostomy, hepaticojejunostomy, and gastrojejunostomy appear intact. No ascites. Impression: No evidence of recurrent or metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma. Cancer status: Absent. CA 19-9: 14 U/mL (normal). Findings consistent with disease-free status following multimodality treatment.

documents.text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY — ESTABLISHED PATIENT VISIT
Mid-Treatment Reassessment: Neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX, Cycle 4 of 8
Borderline Resectable Pancreatic Head Adenocarcinoma

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 66-year-old retired pipe fitter presenting for his scheduled mid-treatment reassessment following completion of four cycles of neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX for borderline resectable pancreatic head adenocarcinoma. He is here today to review restaging imaging, discuss laboratory results, and plan the second half of his neoadjuvant regimen.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. Kowalski was diagnosed approximately two months ago with a 3.8 cm borderline resectable pancreatic head ductal adenocarcinoma (cT3N1M0, Stage III per AJCC 8th edition). At the time of diagnosis, his CA 19-9 was markedly elevated at 1200 U/mL and bilirubin was elevated at 2.1 mg/dL requiring biliary decompression via ERCP with plastic stent placement prior to initiating systemic therapy. Molecular profiling from his EUS-guided FNA specimen revealed a KRAS G12V driver mutation, TP53 p.R248W mutation, SMAD4 homozygous deletion, and CDKN2A homozygous deletion — a genomic profile that, while lacking actionable targeted therapy, underscores the aggressive biology of his tumor. Microsatellite status was confirmed stable (MSS), and the tumor is HRD-negative with BRCA1/2 and ATM germline testing having been ordered at our last encounter.

Following multidisciplinary tumor board consensus, Mr. Kowalski was initiated on neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX — oxali 85 mg/m2, Camptosar 180 mg/m2, leucovorin 400 mg/m2, and 5-FU 2400 mg/m2 over 46 hours — every two weeks, with prophylactic pegfilgrastim on day 2 of each cycle. He has now completed cycles 1 through 4 without any treatment delays. Restaging CT with pancreatic protocol was obtained after cycle 4 and is reviewed below.

From a tolerability standpoint, Mr. Kowalski has experienced several treatment-related side effects that have been managed conservatively. Cycles 1 and 2 were complicated by grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy, grade 2 fatigue, grade 1-2 nausea, and grade 1 alopecia. After cycle 2, he developed grade 2 diarrhea that responded well to loperamide titration and dietary modification (low-fat, low-fiber diet, small frequent meals). No dose reductions were required during cycles 1-4. He has experienced approximately 3 kg of weight loss since initiating therapy, which he attributes to reduced appetite and changes in bowel habits. He denies any new symptoms today that would preclude continuation of therapy.

He reports that the neuropathy has been persistent — a bothersome, predominantly distal tingling and paresthesias in bilateral fingertips and feet — though he acknowledges it has not significantly interfered with his daily activities or his ability to button a shirt or tie his shoes. He has not noted any falls or balance difficulty. The fatigue he describes as moderate — he can perform all activities of daily living and takes one or two brief rest periods during the day. Nausea has been well controlled with ondansetron taken preemptively on infusion days.

Today's restaging CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol demonstrates meaningful interval response: the pancreatic head mass has decreased from 3.8 cm to 3.1 cm, representing approximately an 18% reduction in greatest dimension. Perhaps more importantly from a surgical standpoint, the degree of SMA abutment has improved from 120-150 degrees of circumference to now less than 90 degrees, with the fat plane between the tumor and the SMA appearing at least partially reconstituted. The SMV/portal vein confluence contact remains, without new venous occlusion. The suspicious periportal lymph nodes have also decreased in size from the largest measuring 1.2 cm down to 0.8 cm. There are no new hepatic lesions and no peritoneal disease. Biochemically, the CA 19-9 has declined dramatically from 1200 U/mL at baseline to 380 U/mL — a 68% reduction — which is highly encouraging and consistent with radiographic response.

Overall, the tumor is responding meaningfully to FOLFIRINOX, and we are cautiously optimistic about the trajectory toward surgical resectability.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
- Constitutional: Moderate fatigue, 3 kg weight loss since start of chemotherapy. Denies fever, chills, or night sweats.
- HEENT: Mild mucositis was noted transiently after cycle 2 but has fully resolved. No oral ulcers today.
- Gastrointestinal: Grade 2 diarrhea improved significantly with loperamide and dietary adjustments; currently grade 1 at most. Mild early satiety. No nausea at rest today. Denies melena, hematemesis, or hematochezia. Normal stool color. Denies new abdominal pain.
- Neurological: Persistent bilateral distal sensory neuropathy in fingers and toes, grade 2 by CTCAE. No motor weakness. No gait instability.
- Dermatologic: Mild alopecia (grade 1), stable. No rash or skin breakdown.
- Hematologic: No bleeding events. No bruising. No signs of infection.
- Musculoskeletal: Generalized deconditioning. Mild lower back discomfort, pre-existing.
- Genitourinary: No symptoms. Urination adequate. No dysuria.
- Psychiatric: Mood is anxious but engaged. He has been in communication with social work regarding his prognosis and the possibility of surgery.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
Vital signs: Blood pressure 128/76 mmHg, heart rate 74 bpm, respiratory rate 16/min, temperature 36.7°C, oxygen saturation 97% on room air. Weight: 82.4 kg (down from 85.5 kg at treatment initiation, down 3.1 kg). BMI: 26.2 kg/m2.

General: Alert and oriented. Well-appearing gentleman, mildly fatigued-appearing but comfortable and in no acute distress. Mild temporal wasting noted. Alopecia present (grade 1 — diffuse thinning).

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Sclera anicteric. No jaundice. Mucous membranes moist. Oral cavity: no active mucositis, no ulcerations. No lymphadenopathy in cervical or supraclavicular chains.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheeze, crackles, or rhonchi.

Abdomen: Soft, mildly distended, non-tender to palpation. No guarding or rebound. No hepatomegaly. No splenomegaly appreciated. No palpable masses. Biliary stent site without surrounding tenderness. Bowel sounds present and active. No ascites clinically.

Extremities: No lower extremity edema. Peripheral pulses intact. Bilateral distal extremity sensation testing: diminished light touch and vibratory sense in bilateral toes and fingertips consistent with grade 2 sensory peripheral neuropathy. Monofilament testing: mildly reduced at bilateral great toes. Fine motor testing: intact (buttons shirt without difficulty today).

Skin: Mild diffuse hair thinning. No rash, petechiae, or ecchymoses. No palmar or plantar erythema.

Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Strength 5/5 in bilateral upper and lower extremities. Gait steady.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS
Reviewed from labs drawn prior to today's visit:

- WBC: 5.8 x 10^9/L (normal)
- ANC: 3.4 x 10^9/L (normal; prior nadir approximately 1.2 x 10^9/L during early cycles, now recovered)
- Hemoglobin: 11.2 g/dL (mild anemia, grade 1; stable)
- Platelets: 214 x 10^9/L (normal)
- Sodium: 139 mEq/L | Potassium: 4.0 mEq/L | Bicarbonate: 24 mEq/L | Chloride: 103 mEq/L
- BUN: 14 mg/dL | Creatinine: 0.98 mg/dL | eGFR: >60 mL/min/1.73m2
- Total bilirubin: 0.9 mg/dL (normalized post-biliary stenting; was 2.1 at diagnosis)
- AST: 31 U/L | ALT: 37 U/L | Alkaline phosphatase: 88 U/L (all within normal limits)
- Albumin: 3.5 g/dL (low-normal, consistent with nutritional stress)
- Glucose: 138 mg/dL (elevated; diabetes management to be addressed below)
- HbA1c (drawn at cycle 3): 7.8% (moderately elevated; metformin held on chemotherapy days per institutional protocol)
- CA 19-9: 380 U/mL (down from 1200 U/mL at baseline — 68% reduction)
- CEA: 3.1 ng/mL (improving from 4.8 at baseline)
- Magnesium: 0.78 mmol/L (borderline low; likely related to chemotherapy and diarrhea) — replete today with oral magnesium oxide

---

IMAGING RESULTS
Restaging CT Abdomen/Pelvis with Pancreatic Protocol (triphasic) obtained after cycle 4, reviewed in detail:

MULTIDISCIPLINARY TUMOR BOARD DISCUSSION AND ASSESSMENT:

Medical Oncology perspective: Mr. Kowalski has had an outstanding response to neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX. The CA 19-9 decline from 1200 to 85 U/mL — a 93% reduction — is among the most favorable prognostic markers we can see in this disease. Primary tumor downsizing from 3.8 cm to 2.4 cm and normalization of regional lymph nodes are highly encouraging. His molecular profile (KRAS G12V, TP53, SMAD4 deletion, CDKN2A deletion, MSS, HRD-negative) placed him in a biologically unfavorable subgroup at diagnosis; SMAD4 loss in particular is associated with higher risk of systemic failure, yet he has had no distant progression. The HRD-negative status means PARP inhibitors would not be appropriate as maintenance in the adjuvant setting, and the MSS status means checkpoint immunotherapy would not be expected to benefit him. His response has been achieved solely with cytotoxic chemotherapy, which is gratifying. Oxaliplatin-based therapy has been completed and should not be resumed given cumulative neuropathy (currently grade 2–3); any future regimen should be oxaliplatin-free.

HPB Surgery perspective: Based on the radiographic and biochemical response documented, the surgical team believes that resection is now feasible with a reasonable expectation of achieving R0 (negative margin) resection. The improved SMA fat plane on post-cycle-8 imaging is particularly important — arterial involvement was our primary concern at baseline. The SMV will likely require intraoperative assessment and possibly short-segment resection with primary reconstruction, but this is a well-established maneuver at our institution performed routinely. Intraoperative frozen sections of the pancreatic neck and retroperitoneal margins will be critical. The patient's ECOG PS 1 and adequate nutritional status make him an appropriate surgical candidate. We recommend a planned approach with vascular surgery on standby for portal vein/SMV reconstruction if needed.

Radiation Oncology perspective: Before proceeding directly to surgery, the radiation oncology team recommends a period of consolidative chemoradiation. The rationale is to provide further locoregional therapy to the vascular margin zones — particularly the SMA margin, which remains the most challenging zone for achieving adequate clearance in pancreatic head tumors — prior to surgery. A standard long-course approach using IMRT to 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions (1.8 Gy/fraction) over approximately 5.5 weeks with concurrent capecitabine (Xeloda) as a radiosensitizer is recommended. This approach is supported by institutional experience and is consistent with the sequencing used in several cooperative group neoadjuvant protocols. Capecitabine dosing of 825 mg/m2 BID on radiation days is standard. The radiation field would include the gross tumor volume, the operative bed, and regional at-risk nodal stations (celiac axis, SMA, portal/SMV). We would plan to restage with pancreatic protocol CT approximately 6 weeks after completion of chemoradiation before proceeding with surgical scheduling.

Radiology perspective (imaging-based downstaging): The radiologist confirms that the imaging findings are consistent with significant response and likely downstaging. Importantly, while the tumor has clearly shrunk and the vascular anatomy appears more favorable, we should not interpret this as meaning the tumor is microscopically negative at the vascular margin — desmoplastic reaction and treatment-related fibrosis can obscure the true extent of residual viable tumor at the margins. This is a well-known limitation of post-treatment imaging in pancreatic adenocarcinoma and underscores the importance of careful surgical technique and intraoperative frozen sections.

Nutrition: The patient has lost 7 kg from baseline, with some of this being pre-diagnosis and some treatment-related. Albumin is 3.5 g/dL. He is on pancreatic enzyme replacement. Oral nutritional supplementation should continue throughout chemoradiation; if oral intake deteriorates during radiation, early enteral support via nasojejunal tube should be considered. Close nutritional monitoring will be important preoperatively.

Palliative Care: Not formally engaged at this visit. Patient maintains ECOG PS 1 and goals of care remain curative in intent. Palliative care remains available for symptom management.

The patient and his medical oncologist are seeking surgical evaluation for feasibility of resection given excellent biochemical and radiographic response to multimodality neoadjuvant therapy.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Kowalski is a 66-year-old retired pipe fitter with a 30 pack-year smoking history (cessation approximately 15 years prior), type 2 diabetes mellitus, and hypertension who initially presented with new-onset obstructive jaundice and epigastric discomfort. Workup at that time revealed a 3.8 cm hypovascular mass in the head of the pancreas on pancreatic protocol CT. This was confirmed to be invasive ductal adenocarcinoma by EUS-guided fine needle aspiration. Molecular profiling demonstrated a KRAS G12V-driven tumor with co-occurring TP53 p.R248W mutation, SMAD4 homozygous deletion, and CDKN2A deletion. Microsatellite stable, HRD-negative. At diagnosis, CA 19-9 was markedly elevated at 1200 U/mL.

The tumor was classified as borderline resectable at initial staging given abutment of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) over approximately 120-150 degrees of circumference and short-segment abutment/involvement of the superior mesenteric vein-portal vein confluence (SMV). Two periportal lymph nodes up to 1.2 cm were radiographically suspicious. Clinical stage at diagnosis: cT3N1M0 (Stage III per AJCC 8th edition). Biliary decompression was performed via ERCP with plastic biliary stent placement given hyperbilirubinemia (total bilirubin 2.1 mg/dL at presentation).

Following multidisciplinary tumor board discussion, the patient proceeded with neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX (oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2, irinotecan 180 mg/m2, leucovorin 400 mg/m2, 5-FU bolus and infusional). He received 8 cycles over approximately 4 months. The course was complicated by cumulative grade 2-3 peripheral sensory neuropathy (requiring oxaliplatin dose reduction to 80% in cycles 5-8), grade 3 diarrhea after cycle 7 (requiring brief hospitalization, irinotecan dose reduction to 75%), and grade 2 fatigue. Total weight loss from baseline over the chemotherapy period was approximately 7 kg. He achieved a significant partial response: mid-treatment (after cycle 4) CA 19-9 had fallen to 380 U/mL, and at the end of 8 cycles CA 19-9 was 85 U/mL. Imaging after cycle 8 showed the mass had reduced to 2.4 cm, with improved SMA abutment (now less than 90 degrees), improved SMV/PV interface, and normalization of regional lymph nodes (largest now 0.6 cm).

Following multidisciplinary reassessment, the patient underwent consolidative chemoradiation: intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) to 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions with concurrent capecitabine (825 mg/m2 BID on radiation days, later dose-reduced to 750 mg/m2 BID for grade 1-2 hand-foot syndrome). He completed the full course without treatment interruption. Toxicities during chemoradiation included grade 2 nausea and vomiting, grade 2 fatigue, grade 1 radiation dermatitis, and grade 1 transaminase elevation that resolved spontaneously.

Pre-surgical restaging CT (approximately 6 weeks after completion of chemoradiation) demonstrated further reduction of the pancreatic head mass to 2.1 cm. There is a hypodense central low-attenuation component suggesting treatment-related necrosis. The SMA fat plane appears largely restored with abutment now estimated at less than 60 degrees or possibly no residual contact. The SMV/PV confluence appears patent without definite tumor encasement. Regional lymph nodes are within normal size limits. No distant metastatic disease identified. CA 19-9 at the time of that scan was 62 U/mL — a greater than 95% reduction from baseline.

The patient is referred now for surgical planning for pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure). He is anxious but motivated and has been counseled in detail about the risks and expected recovery. He understands the diagnosis and the intent of surgery.

ASSESSMENT

Mr. Kowalski is a 66-year-old male with pancreatic head ductal adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12V/TP53/SMAD4/CDKN2A-mutated, MSS, HRD-negative, germline BRCA/ATM negative) initially classified as borderline resectable (cT3N1M0) who has achieved an excellent and sustained response to neoadjuvant multimodality therapy. CA 19-9 has declined from 1200 to 62 U/mL (>95% reduction). Radiographic tumor regression from 3.8 cm to 2.1 cm with substantial improvement in vascular abutment. In my surgical judgment, this patient has been sufficiently downstaged and is now an appropriate candidate for curative-intent pancreaticoduodenectomy.

Key surgical considerations:
1. SMA involvement: Based on current imaging, residual SMA abutment is minimal (possibly < 60 degrees or absent). I anticipate complete medial SMA dissection is achievable without arterial resection or reconstruction. SMA-first approach will be used to assess true resectability at the outset.
2. SMV/PV involvement: While the confluence appears patent, there is likely desmoplastic adhesion given the initial involvement. We must be prepared for segmental SMV resection and primary reconstruction (end-to-end anastomosis) with primary interposition graft if necessary. Vascular surgery will be on standby.
3. Margins: The significant neoadjuvant response suggests favorable margin status; however, given initial vascular involvement, close margins remain possible, particularly at the SMV groove and posterior surfaces. We will obtain intraoperative frozen sections of the pancreatic neck margin.
4. Performance status: ECOG PS 1. Mild anemia (Hgb 11.4) — will optimize pre-operatively with iron supplementation and nutritional support. Albumin 3.6 — reasonable but would benefit from continued nutritional optimization. Cardiac clearance obtained (pharmacologic stress test negative for ischemia). Pulmonary function adequate for major surgery.
5. Diabetes: Insulin-dependent diabetes management will be coordinated with endocrinology and anesthesia. Blood sugar targets perioperatively established.
6. Neuropathy: Pre-existing grade 1-2 peripheral neuropathy noted — anesthesia team aware for positioning and monitoring.
7. Biliary stent: Current plastic stent in place. Plan to remove and replace with metal stent prior to surgery if significant intrahepatic biliary dilation persists or if surgery is delayed further, to reduce the risk of cholangitis. Alternatively, we will proceed directly to resection and remove the stent at the time of surgery given adequate bilirubin and liver function.

---

PLAN

1. Proceed with open pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) as planned. Standard reconstruction: duct-to-mucosa pancreaticojejunostomy, hepaticojejunostomy, antecolic gastrojejunostomy. Pylorus-preserving versus standard Whipple to be determined at time of surgery based on duodenal involvement.

2. Vascular surgery consultation confirmed and placed on standby for potential SMV segmental resection and reconstruction. Pre-op vascular surgery assessment arranged.

3. Pre-operative optimization:
   - Oral nutritional supplementation continued (high-protein, high-calorie formula)
   - Iron supplementation initiated (ferrous sulfate 325 mg BID) to address mild pre-operative anemia; recheck CBC in 3 weeks
   - Perioperative insulin management plan discussed with anesthesia and endocrinology
   - Continue lisinopril up to day prior to surgery; hold on day of surgery
   - Hold metformin (was already discontinued during treatment; not currently on it)
   - Continue Creon with all meals up to and including pre-operative period
   - Pre-operative bowel prep per institutional protocol for pancreaticoduodenectomy

4. Anesthesia pre-assessment completed; cleared for major abdominal surgery under general endotracheal anesthesia with epidural analgesia (thoracic epidural for post-operative pain management).

5. Consent obtained: Patient and spouse met with me today and were counseled extensively regarding risks of pancreaticoduodenectomy including: mortality (< 3% at high-volume center), pancreatic fistula and leak (20-30%), delayed gastric emptying (15-20%), bile leak, wound infection, intraabdominal abscess, need for transfusion, need for vascular reconstruction, conversion to exploratory laparotomy only if widespread peritoneal disease found at exploration (unlikely given no imaging evidence), venous thromboembolism, and longer-term issues including new or worsening pancreatic exocrine and endocrine insufficiency. Patient verbalized understanding and signed informed consent documents including consent for potential SMV/PV resection and reconstruction.

6. Patient instructed to continue current medication regimen as above until day of surgery (with exceptions noted).

7. Surgery scheduled. Patient to present for pre-operative nursing assessment and labs the morning of the procedure. NPO after midnight the night before.

8. Anticipated hospital stay: 7-10 days for uncomplicated course; longer if vascular reconstruction required or if post-operative complications arise.

9. Post-operative plan: Standard Whipple recovery pathway. Post-op drain amylase checked day 3. CA 19-9 post-operatively at 4 weeks. Oncology follow-up arranged for adjuvant therapy planning (target initiation at 6-8 weeks post-operatively pending recovery).

Patient expressed understanding and is agreeable to proceed. He appears motivated, well-supported by family, and has realistic expectations about recovery. He understands that while imaging and biochemistry are encouraging, the final pathological assessment — including margin status and lymph node involvement — will help guide further adjuvant decision-making.

COMMENT

This pancreaticoduodenectomy specimen demonstrates moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma with extensive treatment-related fibrosis and necrosis in the tumor bed following neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX chemotherapy and chemoradiation. The histomorphologic findings are consistent with CAP tumor regression grade 2 (moderate response), indicating significant but incomplete pathologic response to multimodality neoadjuvant therapy.

All surgical margins are free of carcinoma (R0 resection). Residual viable carcinoma measures 2.0 cm. Regional nodal metastases are present (3/18 nodes), with treatment effect also evident within the positive nodal deposits. Perineural invasion and lymphovascular invasion are identified. Adventitial involvement of the resected SMV segment is noted without intraluminal extension.

IHC confirms loss of SMAD4/DPC4 expression and aberrant p53 overexpression, consistent with the mutational profile identified on prior EUS-FNA molecular testing (KRAS G12V / TP53 p.R248W / SMAD4 homozygous deletion / CDKN2A homozygous deletion). Mismatch repair proteins are intact (MMR-proficient), consistent with microsatellite stable designation. HER2 is not amplified.

Germline BRCA1/2 and ATM testing results are pending and will be reported as an addendum upon receipt.

Results discussed with surgical team. Clinical correlation recommended.

CYTOLOGIC FINDINGS
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
The smears are highly cellular and show cohesive three-dimensional clusters, crowded sheets, and occasional single atypical epithelial cells set against a background of hemorrhage, proteinaceous material, and scattered desmoplastic stromal fragments. The stromal fragments are composed of dense, hypocellular fibrous tissue, a characteristic feature of the reactive desmoplastic stroma associated with pancreatobiliary-type adenocarcinoma.

The neoplastic cells are large and polygonal to columnar, with markedly enlarged, pleomorphic nuclei. Nuclear features are unequivocal for malignancy and include:
  - Marked nuclear enlargement with nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio greater than 0.7
  - Irregular, angulated nuclear contours with membrane irregularities
  - Coarse, clumped, and unevenly distributed chromatin
  - Conspicuous macronucleoli, often eccentrically placed, present in the majority of cells
  - Marked anisonucleosis (4:1 or greater variation in nuclear size within individual clusters)
  - Occasional nuclear molding
  - Rare mitotic figures identified

Cytoplasmic features:
  - Moderate to abundant pale-to-vacuolated cytoplasm; occasional intracytoplasmic mucin vacuoles present
  - Distinct cell borders where cells form acinar or gland-like arrangements in the three-dimensional clusters
  - Some cells with wispy, ill-defined cytoplasmic tails

Architectural features:
  - Disorganized crowded clusters with loss of honeycomb architecture
  - Sheet-like arrangements with nuclear overlapping
  - Occasional rosette-like or acinar structures
  - Three-dimensional "drunken honeycomb" pattern in some clusters
  - "Picket fence" nuclear palisading at the periphery of some fragments

Background:
  - Desmoplastic stromal fragments (fibrous bands, elongated spindle cells) present — a hallmark of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma
  - Rare benign ductal epithelial cells for comparison; the malignant cells show clear-cut morphologic distinction from background benign elements
  - No significant necrosis identified
  - No acinar cell or islet cell features

Cell block sections confirm the above findings. H&E-stained sections of the cell block show infiltrating glandular structures composed of the above-described malignant cells embedded within desmoplastic stroma, closely recapitulating a ductal adenocarcinoma morphology. Gland lumina are irregular and lined by atypical cells.

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Mr. Raymond Kowalski, a 67-year-old male, presents to the hepatopancreatobiliary surgery clinic for his two-week postoperative follow-up after undergoing an open pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) with segmental superior mesenteric vein (SMV) resection and primary reconstruction for pancreatic head adenocarcinoma. He reports fatigue, mild epigastric discomfort, and decreased appetite. He was discharged from the hospital approximately two days ago with a closed-suction abdominal drain in situ.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mr. Kowalski has a well-documented history of borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) of the pancreatic head, initially diagnosed approximately seven months ago at age 66. At diagnosis, his CA 19-9 was markedly elevated at 1,200 U/mL, and cross-sectional imaging demonstrated a 3.8 cm hypovascular mass with abutment of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) over approximately 120–150 degrees and short-segment abutment of the SMV-portal vein confluence, consistent with borderline resectable disease by NCCN/AHPBA criteria. Two periportal lymph nodes were radiographically suspicious. Molecular profiling of the EUS-FNA specimen revealed KRAS G12V, TP53 R248W, SMAD4 deletion, and CDKN2A homozygous deletion; the tumor was microsatellite stable, HRD-negative, and TMB-low with no actionable targeted therapy identified.

He was initially treated with biliary decompression via ERCP with plastic biliary stent placement given obstructive jaundice (total bilirubin 2.1 mg/dL). Following multidisciplinary tumor board discussion, he underwent neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX (oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2, irinotecan 180 mg/m2, leucovorin 400 mg/m2, 5-FU 2400 mg/m2 over 46 hours) for 8 cycles over approximately 4 months, with an oxaliplatin dose reduction to 68 mg/m2 after cycle 4 due to cumulative grade 2–3 peripheral sensory neuropathy, and irinotecan reduction to 135 mg/m2 (75% dose) after cycle 7 due to a grade 3 diarrhea episode requiring hospitalization. CA 19-9 declined dramatically from 1,200 U/mL at baseline to 85 U/mL after cycle 8, and restaging pancreatic protocol CT showed tumor reduction from 3.8 cm to 2.4 cm with improved vascular abutment.

Following the tumor board reassessment confirming excellent biochemical and radiographic response, he proceeded to consolidative long-course chemoradiation: IMRT to 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions with concurrent capecitabine (reduced to 750 mg/m2 BID due to grade 1–2 hand-foot syndrome). Pre-surgical restaging CT after chemoradiation showed further tumor reduction to 2.1 cm, essentially restored SMA fat plane with estimated residual abutment under 60 degrees, and patent SMV-PV confluence. CA 19-9 at that point was 62 U/mL.

He was brought to the operating room and underwent open pancreaticoduodenectomy. Intraoperatively, the SMA was successfully dissected free via complete medial dissection without requiring arterial reconstruction. The SMV demonstrated a short segment of desmoplastic adhesion, necessitating segmental SMV resection with primary end-to-end reconstruction, performed with vascular surgery assistance. No peritoneal metastases or gross hepatic metastases were identified. Intraoperative frozen sections of the pancreatic neck margin were negative. Reconstruction was performed in standard fashion: duct-to-mucosa pancreaticojejunostomy, hepaticojejunostomy, and gastrojejunostomy. Estimated blood loss was 650 mL; two units of packed red blood cells were transfused intraoperatively. Total operative time was 6 hours 45 minutes.

Final surgical pathology confirmed: 2.0 cm residual moderately differentiated ductal adenocarcinoma with extensive fibrosis and treatment effect (CAP tumor response grade 2, moderate response). All surgical margins were negative (R0 resection): pancreatic neck margin > 1 mm, posterior margin 1.5 mm, anterior margin 2.1 mm, SMV groove margin 1.0 mm. Lymph node status: 3/18 nodes positive for metastatic adenocarcinoma (pN1). Perineural invasion and lymphovascular invasion both present. Pathologic staging: ypT2N1M0, Stage IIB (AJCC 8th edition, post-treatment).

Postoperative hospital course: He was in the surgical ICU for the first two days, then transferred to the surgical floor. His diet was advanced from clear liquids to a soft diet by postoperative day 5 without significant difficulty. He developed delayed gastric emptying (grade A per ISGPS criteria), managed conservatively with nasogastric decompression and metoclopramide, which resolved by approximately postoperative day 10. Drain amylase checked on postoperative day 3 was elevated at 1,850 U/L (greater than three times the upper limit of normal), raising concern for early pancreatic fistula formation, and the drain was accordingly left in situ. He was discharged on postoperative day 12 with the drain in place and is returning today for wound inspection, drain assessment, and discussion of next steps including adjuvant therapy planning.

CT CHEST / ABDOMEN / PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
Post-Whipple Surveillance — First Post-Adjuvant Completion Study

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 67-year-old male with a history of borderline resectable pancreatic head ductal adenocarcinoma (cT3N1M0 at presentation, KRAS G12V / TP53 / SMAD4 / CDKN2A-mutated, microsatellite stable) who completed the following multimodality treatment sequence: neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX for eight cycles with documented partial response, followed by consolidative long-course chemoradiation (IMRT 50.4 Gy in 28 fractions with concurrent capecitabine), followed by open pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) with segmental superior mesenteric vein resection and primary end-to-end reconstruction, achieving an R0 resection (ypT2N1M0, Stage IIB; CAP response grade 2; 3 of 18 lymph nodes positive). Postoperative course was complicated by a Grade B pancreatic fistula, subsequently resolved. He then completed six cycles of adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine per the APACT regimen. This examination is obtained as the first post-adjuvant completion surveillance study, approximately 16 months after initial diagnosis. Serum CA 19-9 at time of scan: 14 U/mL (within reference range).

TECHNIQUE:
Multiphase CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed on a 64-slice multi-detector CT scanner following the administration of 100 mL of nonionic iodinated intravenous contrast (iohexol) injected at 3.5 mL/sec. Imaging was acquired in the portal venous phase for the chest and in pancreatic protocol triphasic fashion (late arterial, portal venous, and delayed phases) for the abdomen and pelvis, supplemented by coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformations. Oral contrast was not administered given the patient's post-Whipple anatomy and history of delayed gastric emptying. Tube voltage: 120 kVp, automated tube current modulation. Slice thickness: 2.5 mm axial, 3.0 mm coronal/sagittal reformats.

COMPARISON:
Prior CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast obtained approximately three months after the Whipple procedure (mid-adjuvant therapy), and the pre-surgical restaging CT obtained approximately six weeks after completion of chemoradiation. Selected comparison with the initial diagnostic pancreatic protocol CT obtained at the time of diagnosis.

FINDINGS:

CHEST:
Lungs and airways: The lungs are well aerated bilaterally. No pulmonary nodules, masses, or areas of consolidation are identified. No ground-glass opacities. The airways are patent centrally and peripherally to the extent visualized. No endobronchial lesions.

Pleural spaces: No pleural effusions. No pleural thickening or pleural-based nodularity to suggest metastatic seeding.

Mediastinum and hila: The mediastinum is midline. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes. No anterior mediastinal mass. The heart is normal in size without pericardial effusion. The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber. The visualized pulmonary vasculature is unremarkable.

Chest wall: No suspicious osseous lesions. No chest wall soft tissue masses.

ABDOMEN:

Surgical bed / pancreatic region: The expected post-pancreaticoduodenectomy anatomy is identified. The pancreatic remnant (body and tail) is visible in the left upper quadrant with mild parenchymal atrophy, a sequela of the prior Whipple resection and known exocrine insufficiency, unchanged from prior study. The pancreaticojejunostomy anastomosis appears patent and intact without peripancreatic fluid collection, abscess, or perianastomotic air-fluid collection. There is no discrete soft tissue mass, nodular enhancement, or focal lesion in the surgical bed to suggest locoregional tumor recurrence. Mild residual post-treatment and postoperative fibrofatty stranding in the mesenteric root and perivascular soft tissues persists, stable to mildly improved compared to the prior examination; this appearance is consistent with expected post-treatment inflammatory/fibrotic change and does not have imaging features concerning for recurrence. The celiac axis, superior mesenteric artery, and hepatic artery origins are patent without stenosis or perivascular soft tissue encasement. The superior mesenteric vein at the site of prior segmental resection and end-to-end anastomosis is patent with no evidence of thrombosis, stenosis, or recurrent tumor encasement; a trace amount of perianastomotic soft tissue is present, stable, consistent with postoperative scar. The portal vein and splenic vein are patent and normal in caliber. No new peripancreatic or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.

Liver: The liver is normal in size with homogeneous parenchymal attenuation. No focal hepatic lesions are identified. No hypervascular or hypodense lesions suspicious for metastatic deposits on the arterial or portal venous phase images. The portal and hepatic veins are patent. No biliary ductal dilatation. The hepaticojejunostomy anastomosis appears intact with no upstream biliary obstruction. Mild post-surgical pneumobilia from prior biliary-enteric anastomosis is noted, unchanged from prior imaging and expected in this surgical context.

Gallbladder: Absent — status post cholecystectomy as part of the Whipple procedure.

The visualized osseous structures, including the thoracic and lumbar spine, ribs, sternum, pelvis, and proximal femora, are without aggressive lytic or blastic lesions. Mild multilevel degenerative changes are noted in the lumbar spine. No pathologic fractures.

IMPRESSION:

1. No evidence of locoregional recurrence. The surgical bed following pancreaticoduodenectomy appears clear, with stable post-treatment fibrofatty changes and no suspicious soft tissue nodularity, enhancement, or mass formation in the region of the prior pancreatic head tumor.

2. No hepatic metastases. The liver enhances homogeneously without focal lesions on this multiphasic examination.

3. No pulmonary metastases or suspicious pulmonary nodules.

4. No peritoneal carcinomatosis or ascites.

5. No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes in any visualized nodal station, including the mediastinum, retroperitoneum, or mesentery.

Clinical correlation with serum CA 19-9 (reported at 14 U/mL at the time of this examination, within normal limits) supports the absence of recurrent or metastatic disease.

CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH PANCREATIC PROTOCOL (TRIPHASIC)

CLINICAL INDICATION: 66-year-old male with new diagnosis of pancreatic head mass, EUS-FNA confirming adenocarcinoma. Pancreatic protocol CT obtained for surgical staging and vascular mapping. Evaluate resectability. Elevated serum CA 19-9. History of type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the abdomen and pelvis performed with IV contrast (120 mL iohexol, 350 mg I/mL) following a pancreatic protocol with three acquisition phases: non-contrast, pancreatic parenchymal phase (35-second delay), and portal venous phase (70-second delay). Thin-section 0.6 mm axial reconstructions performed with multiplanar reformats in coronal and sagittal planes. Curved planar reformations of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA), superior mesenteric vein (SMV), portal vein, and celiac axis were generated. Water administered orally prior to examination for gastric/duodenal opacification.

COMPARISON: No prior cross-sectional abdominal imaging on file.

---

FINDINGS:

PANCREAS:
There is an ill-defined, poorly marginated, hypovascular mass centered in the head and uncinate process of the pancreas measuring approximately 3.8 cm in greatest axial dimension (craniocaudal extent approximately 3.2 cm). On the pancreatic parenchymal phase, the mass demonstrates markedly reduced enhancement relative to the surrounding pancreatic parenchyma, appearing as a hypodense infiltrative lesion with irregular, spiculated margins and associated desmoplastic reaction. There is no discrete capsule. The mass does not demonstrate appreciable internal calcification. The remaining pancreatic body and tail demonstrate moderate parenchymal atrophy, attributable to upstream obstruction. The pancreatic duct in the body and tail is dilated, measuring up to 6 mm in diameter, consistent with obstructive ductal dilation from the mass. No additional pancreatic lesions are identified.

VASCULAR RELATIONSHIPS (key findings for surgical planning):

Superior Mesenteric Artery (SMA): The tumor demonstrates direct contact with the right lateral and posterior wall of the SMA over an arc of approximately 120 to 150 degrees of circumference. The fat plane between the tumor and the SMA is obliterated along this segment, however there is no definite irregularity or narrowing of the SMA lumen. The contact spans approximately 1.5 cm in craniocaudal extent. The SMA origin from the aorta is widely patent. The inferior pancreaticoduodenal arcade, while encased in the region of the mass, does not show occlusion.

Superior Mesenteric Vein (SMV) and Portal Vein Confluence: Short-segment contact between the right lateral wall of the SMV and the medial aspect of the tumor is present, spanning approximately 1.0 to 1.2 cm. There is focal loss of the intervening fat plane with subtle irregularity of the SMV wall at this location, raising concern for early mural involvement. The SMV lumen appears patent without definite thrombosis; however, the interface between tumor and vein is indistinct on the parenchymal phase. The confluence of the SMV and portal vein is similarly involved with a short segment of apparent abutment; no venous occlusion or collateral venous development is identified. The main portal vein above the confluence is patent and normal in caliber.

Celiac Axis and Hepatic Artery: The celiac axis origin and common hepatic artery are patent and widely separated from the tumor. No tumor contact with the celiac axis or its branches. The proper hepatic artery, left hepatic artery, and right hepatic artery all demonstrate normal caliber and course. A replaced right hepatic artery is NOT identified. No accessory hepatic arterial anatomy of surgical consequence is noted.

Aorta and Inferior Vena Cava: The abdominal aorta is mildly ectatic with mild atherosclerotic calcification, without aneurysmal dilation. The inferior vena cava is patent. No aortocaval adenopathy.

BILIARY SYSTEM:
The common bile duct is mildly dilated, measuring up to 10 mm in the distal common bile duct just proximal to its entry into the pancreatic head mass. The intrahepatic bile ducts are borderline prominent bilaterally, consistent with mild proximal biliary obstruction. No cholelithiasis or choledocholithiasis identified. The gallbladder is present, nondistended, and without intraluminal stones or wall thickening. No pneumobilia.

LYMPH NODES:
Two periportal/hepatoduodenal ligament lymph nodes are identified, the largest measuring approximately 1.2 cm in short axis. These are mildly rounded in morphology and demonstrate slightly increased density on portal venous phase imaging, raising concern for metastatic nodal involvement. There is no bulky adenopathy in the retroperitoneum, mesentery, para-aortic, or aortocaval regions. No pelvic lymphadenopathy.

LIVER:
The liver is normal in size with smooth contours. The hepatic parenchyma is homogeneous in attenuation on all three phases. No focal hepatic lesion is identified. No arterially enhancing lesion, no hypoenhancing mass, and no hepatic cyst. No biloma. No evidence of hepatic metastases.

ADRENAL GLANDS:
Bilateral adrenal glands appear normal in size and morphology. No adrenal nodule or mass.

SPLEEN:
Spleen is normal in size and homogeneous in attenuation. No splenic lesion.

KIDNEYS:
Bilateral kidneys are normal in size and enhancement pattern. No hydronephrosis, renal calculi, or solid renal mass. Bilateral renal cortical cysts measuring up to 1.0 cm, all too small to fully characterize but with simple CT attenuation characteristics.

STOMACH AND DUODENUM:
The stomach is decompressed and unremarkable. The duodenum is extrinsically compressed by the pancreatic head mass along the medial wall of the second portion (C-loop), with no definite mucosal invasion identified on this examination. No duodenal obstruction.

SMALL BOWEL AND COLON:
No small bowel obstruction. No bowel wall thickening. No mesenteric masses. The colon and rectum appear unremarkable.

PERITONEUM AND MESENTERY:
No peritoneal implants or nodules are identified. No omental caking. No free intraperitoneal fluid or ascites.

BONY STRUCTURES AND SOFT TISSUES:
No lytic or blastic osseous lesions suspicious for metastatic disease. Mild degenerative changes of the lumbar spine. No pathologic fracture. Abdominal wall and soft tissues are unremarkable. No inguinal hernia.

PELVIS (portal venous phase only):
No pelvic mass or free fluid. Bladder is normally distended. Prostate is mildly enlarged, consistent with benign prostatic hypertrophy in this age group.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. ILL-DEFINED HYPOVASCULAR MASS IN THE PANCREATIC HEAD AND UNCINATE PROCESS, measuring approximately 3.8 cm, with imaging features strongly characteristic of ductal adenocarcinoma in the clinical setting of biopsy-confirmed malignancy.

2. BORDERLINE RESECTABLE PANCREATIC HEAD ADENOCARCINOMA by NCCN/AHPBA imaging criteria:
   - SMA: Tumor contact involving approximately 120-150 degrees of circumference without definite luminal irregularity; no arterial encasement exceeding 180 degrees.
   - SMV/Portal Vein Confluence: Short-segment tumor abutment with focal loss of fat plane and possible subtle mural contact; vessel remains patent without occlusion. Venous reconstruction may be required at surgery.

3. OBSTRUCTIVE PANCREATIC AND BILIARY DUCTAL DILATION: Main pancreatic duct dilated to 6 mm in the body and tail; mild common bile duct dilation to 10 mm, consistent with double-duct sign physiology given the location of the mass.

4. TWO SUSPICIOUS PERIPORTAL LYMPH NODES measuring up to 1.2 cm in short axis; morphology and size raise concern for regional nodal metastases, though definitive characterization is not possible on CT alone.

5. NO HEPATIC METASTASES.

6. NO PERITONEAL CARCINOMATOSIS OR ASCITES.

7. NO DISTANT METASTATIC DISEASE IDENTIFIED ON THIS EXAMINATION.

Overall staging impression: Locally advanced pancreatic head adenocarcinoma, consistent with cT3N1M0 (Stage III), borderline resectable by current imaging criteria. Vascular map provided above for surgical planning purposes. Correlation with serum CA 19-9 and multidisciplinary hepatopancreatobiliary tumor board evaluation recommended prior to definitive management decision.

PET/CT WHOLE BODY (18F-FDG) — STAGING

CLINICAL INDICATION:
66-year-old male with recently diagnosed pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, head of pancreas, classified as borderline resectable per NCCN/AHPBA criteria on prior pancreatic protocol CT (3.8 cm mass with SMA abutment < 180 degrees and short-segment SMV-portal vein confluence involvement). EUS-guided FNA confirmed adenocarcinoma. Serum CA 19-9 markedly elevated at 1200 U/mL. Study performed for metabolic staging to exclude occult distant metastatic disease prior to treatment planning.

TECHNIQUE:
Whole-body PET/CT was performed from the vertex of the skull to the mid-thighs following intravenous administration of approximately 12.4 mCi (459 MBq) of 18F-fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG). The patient fasted for a minimum of 6 hours prior to injection. Serum glucose at time of injection: 118 mg/dL. Uptake period: 62 minutes. CT component performed for attenuation correction and anatomic localization (120 kVp, 80–200 mAs, 3.75 mm slice thickness). No intravenous CT contrast administered. Images were acquired from skull base to mid-thigh. SUV measurements represent standardized uptake values corrected for body weight (SUVmax).

COMPARISON:
CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol (triphasic), same admission.

FINDINGS:

HEAD, NECK, AND CHEST:
No abnormal foci of FDG hypermetabolism are identified in the brain. The salivary glands, thyroid gland, and cervical soft tissues demonstrate physiologic tracer distribution without focal lesions. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy with pathologic FDG uptake. Mediastinum: The cardiomediastinal silhouette appears unremarkable by CT morphology. No mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes with abnormal FDG avidity. Physiologic cardiac and great vessel blood pool activity is noted. Lungs: The lung parenchyma is clear bilaterally. There are no pulmonary nodules or masses. No pleural effusions. No foci of abnormal pulmonary FDG uptake. Mild linear atelectasis in the right lower lobe is noted on CT, without corresponding metabolic activity.

ABDOMEN:
Primary Tumor — Pancreatic Head:
There is a prominently hypermetabolic mass centered in the head of the pancreas. The lesion demonstrates markedly elevated FDG avidity with an SUVmax of 8.4. On the co-registered CT images, this corresponds to the known 3.8 cm ill-defined hypovascular mass in the pancreatic head with associated upstream main pancreatic ductal dilatation (approximately 6 mm) and mild intrahepatic and extrahepatic biliary ductal dilation. The intense and heterogeneous glucose uptake is consistent with high metabolic activity of the primary malignancy. No photopenic core to suggest central necrosis on PET, though the lesion is heterogeneous.

Regional Lymph Nodes:
Two mildly FDG-avid lymph nodes are identified in the periportal region. The more anteriorly situated node demonstrates an SUVmax of 3.1, and the second node posterolateral to the hepatoduodenal ligament demonstrates an SUVmax of 2.8. On CT correlation, these correspond to ovoid soft-tissue structures measuring up to approximately 1.0–1.2 cm in short axis, previously noted on the pancreatic protocol CT. The degree of FDG uptake is above expected background for benign reactive nodes in this region, raising concern for nodal metastatic involvement, though SUV values in this range can occasionally be seen with reactive lymphadenopathy in the setting of adjacent tumor inflammation. No other regional lymph node groups (celiac axis, paraaortic, aortocaval, mesenteric) demonstrate pathologic FDG uptake above background.

Liver:
The liver is normal in size and morphology. There are no focal hepatic lesions on CT. Importantly, no foci of abnormal FDG uptake are identified anywhere in the hepatic parenchyma. The background hepatic SUV is within the expected physiologic range (mean liver SUV approximately 2.1). Hepatic arterial and portal venous phase characteristics noted on the separate pancreatic protocol CT confirmed absence of hypervascular or hypovascular lesions.

Gallbladder and Biliary System:
A biliary stent is in place within the common bile duct, consistent with the known history of therapeutic ERCP with plastic stent placement for biliary decompression. There is mild periductal FDG activity immediately adjacent to the stent, which likely reflects post-procedural inflammatory changes rather than malignant involvement. The gallbladder wall is not thickened and demonstrates no abnormal metabolic activity.

Spleen:
Normal in size. No focal splenic lesions. No abnormal FDG uptake.

Adrenal Glands:
Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in size and configuration. No adrenal lesions with pathologic FDG avidity are identified. No adrenal nodules or masses are identified on CT.

Kidneys and Retroperitoneum:
The kidneys are normal in size with expected physiologic urinary tracer excretion. No focal renal masses or abnormal hypermetabolism. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy with pathologic FDG uptake. The aorta and inferior vena cava appear unremarkable.

PERITONEUM AND OMENTUM:
No foci of abnormal FDG activity are detected in the peritoneal cavity, omentum, or mesentery. No peritoneal implants. No ascites identified on CT.

PELVIS:
No pelvic lymphadenopathy with pathologic FDG uptake. Urinary bladder contains excreted tracer (physiologic). Prostate gland is not enlarged. No osseous lesions in the pelvis with abnormal metabolic activity.

SKELETAL SURVEY:
The visualized axial and appendicular skeleton demonstrates no focal osseous lesions with abnormal FDG uptake. No lytic or sclerotic lesions are identified on the CT component that would suggest skeletal metastases. Mild degenerative changes are noted in the lumbar spine, consistent with age.

IMPRESSION:

1. Intensely hypermetabolic mass in the head of the pancreas (SUVmax 8.4), corresponding to the known 3.8 cm pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma on prior CT. The metabolic activity is markedly elevated and consistent with aggressive primary malignancy.

2. Two mildly FDG-avid periportal lymph nodes (SUVmax 3.1 and 2.8), which are metabolically and morphologically suspicious for regional nodal involvement. These nodes correspond to those identified on the pancreatic protocol CT and are consistent with the radiographic staging of cN1 disease.

3. No hepatic lesions with pathologic FDG uptake. The liver is metabolically clear.

4. No evidence of distant metastatic disease. Specifically, no pulmonary metastases, no peritoneal carcinomatosis, no osseous metastases, and no distant nodal disease above the diaphragm.

5. Biliary stent in situ with expected periductal inflammatory activity.

Overall, these findings are consistent with locally advanced pancreatic head adenocarcinoma confined to the primary site and regional lymph nodes, without evidence of distant hematogenous or peritoneal spread. The metabolic staging supports the borderline resectable classification established on prior cross-sectional imaging. The absence of occult distant metastatic disease on this whole-body survey is relevant for treatment planning discussions, including consideration of neoadjuvant systemic therapy.
