1. KRAS — p.G12V (c.35G>T) — Missense Mutation
   Chromosome: 12p12.1
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 42%
   Classification: PATHOGENIC — Oncogenic Driver
   Copy Number: Diploid background
   Clonality: Clonal (high VAF consistent with early trunk driver event)
   Functional Consequence: Constitutive activation of RAS/MAPK signaling pathway; GTPase activity impaired; resistance to intrinsic and GAP-mediated GTP hydrolysis. KRAS p.G12V (Gly→Val substitution) results in persistent GTP-bound, active conformation of KRAS protein, driving uncontrolled cell proliferation, survival, and invasion via RAF/MEK/ERK and PI3K/AKT/mTOR effector cascades.
   Prevalence: KRAS mutations are found in approximately 90-95% of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinomas. The G12V substitution represents approximately 20-25% of all KRAS mutations in PDAC (G12D is most common at ~40%).
   Therapeutic Implications: KRAS G12V mutation does NOT confer eligibility for KRAS G12C inhibitors (sotorasib [Lumakras], adagrasib [Krazati]) — these are cysteine-selective covalent inhibitors and are specifically inactive against the G12V valine substitution. No FDA-approved targeted therapy directly addressing KRAS G12V is currently available. Pan-KRAS inhibitors and KRAS G12V-selective agents remain investigational. Enrollment in KRAS G12V-targeting clinical trials should be considered. Downstream pathway inhibition (MEK inhibitors, ERK inhibitors) has shown limited single-agent activity in PDAC and is not standard of care.
   Evidence Level: Tier I — FDA-recognized oncogenic driver; no approved matched targeted therapy for this specific allele.

2. TP53 — p.R248W (c.742C>T) — Missense Mutation
   Chromosome: 17p13.1
   Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 38%
   Classification: PATHOGENIC — Loss-of-Function / Dominant-Negative
   Copy Number: Concurrent allelic loss (LOH at 17p) — biallelic inactivation presumed
   Functional Consequence: p.R248W is one of the most common and well-characterized TP53 hotspot mutations. The arginine-to-tryptophan substitution at codon 248 disrupts direct DNA contact within the DNA-binding domain, abolishing sequence-specific transcriptional activation of p53 target genes (including p21/CDKN1A, MDM2, PUMA, BAX). Additionally, p.R248W displays dominant-negative activity by co-precipitating with wild-type p53 tetramers, and exhibits gain-of-function oncogenic properties including enhanced cell migration, invasion, and chemoresistance through interactions with p63/p73 family members and chromatin-remodeling complexes.
   Prevalence: TP53 mutations are found in approximately 50-75% of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinomas. The R248W hotspot is among the most frequently encountered.
   Therapeutic Implications: No direct targeted therapy approved for TP53 p.R248W. Strong nuclear p53 IHC staining (gain-of-function mutant pattern) may be used as a surrogate marker in pathologic assessment. Loss of normal p53-mediated DNA damage checkpoint function may theoretically increase sensitivity to DNA-damaging agents (e.g., platinum compounds, irinotecan) — however, this is a general consideration and not a validated predictive biomarker in PDAC.
   Evidence Level: Tier I — Well-established oncogenic driver; no approved matched targeted therapy.

3. SMAD4 (DPC4) — Homozygous Deletion
   Chromosome: 18q21.2
   Copy Number: 0 copies (homozygous loss confirmed; log2 ratio -2.8)
   Classification: PATHOGENIC — Tumor Suppressor Loss
   Functional Consequence: Complete biallelic loss of SMAD4, the central mediator of canonical TGF-β/BMP signaling. SMAD4 loss disrupts TGF-β-mediated cytostatic responses (cell cycle arrest, apoptosis, senescence) and paradoxically promotes TGF-β-driven pro-tumorigenic effects including epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT), invasion, and immunosuppression. In pancreatic cancer, SMAD4 loss is a late-stage event typically occurring after KRAS activation and CDKN2A/TP53 loss, consistent with classic PDAC progression.
   Prevalence: SMAD4 deletions or inactivating mutations are found in approximately 50-60% of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinomas.
   Prognostic Significance: SMAD4 loss is associated with a greater propensity for systemic (distant) metastatic disease as opposed to predominantly locoregional failure. Some retrospective data suggest SMAD4 loss predicts for worse outcomes with locally ablative/radiotherapy approaches, though this remains investigational. SMAD4 status may inform post-resection patterns of failure.
   Therapeutic Implications: No approved targeted therapy. Investigational: TGF-β pathway inhibitors under study. SMAD4 status may be relevant when considering neoadjuvant systemic approaches vs. radiation consolidation strategies.
   Evidence Level: Tier I — Well-established tumor suppressor in PDAC; no approved matched targeted therapy.

4. CDKN2A — Homozygous Deletion
   Chromosome: 9p21.3
   Copy Number: 0 copies (homozygous deletion confirmed; includes both p16/INK4A and p14/ARF reading frames)
   Classification: PATHOGENIC — Tumor Suppressor Loss
   Functional Consequence: CDKN2A encodes two critical tumor suppressor proteins via alternative reading frames: p16/INK4A (CDK4/6 inhibitor — prevents Rb phosphorylation and G1/S cell cycle progression) and p14/ARF (MDM2 inhibitor — stabilizes p53). Homozygous deletion of CDKN2A eliminates both tumor-suppressive functions. Combined loss of p16 (CDKN2A) and TP53 function represents two of the four canonical genetic "hits" in the Vogelstein model of PDAC progression (alongside KRAS activation and SMAD4 loss).
   Prevalence: CDKN2A loss (deletion or promoter hypermethylation) is found in approximately 95% of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinomas — one of the highest frequencies of any cancer type.
   Therapeutic Implications: Theoretically, CDKN2A loss and consequent CDK4/6 dependency might suggest sensitivity to CDK4/6 inhibitors (palbociclib [Ibrance], ribociclib [Kisqali], abemaciclib [Verzenio]). However, KRAS-mutant tumors typically maintain bypass mechanisms through cyclin D1 upregulation and other RB pathway perturbations, and CDK4/6 inhibitors have not demonstrated substantial single-agent activity in KRAS-mutant PDAC in early-phase trials. Combinatorial strategies remain under investigation.
   Evidence Level: Tier II — Established PDAC driver; CDK4/6 inhibitor data insufficient for standard use.

MOLECULAR SUMMARY AND INTERPRETATION
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
This specimen harbors the four canonical somatic alterations that define the classic progression model of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma:

  1. KRAS p.G12V (VAF 42%) — Clonal oncogenic driver; constitutive RAS/MAPK activation
  2. CDKN2A homozygous deletion — Cell cycle deregulation; loss of p16/INK4A and p14/ARF
  3. TP53 p.R248W (VAF 38%) — DNA damage checkpoint abrogation; dominant-negative + gain-of-function
  4. SMAD4 homozygous deletion — TGF-β pathway loss; associated with systemic metastatic phenotype

This molecular profile ("KCST" quad-mutation) is found in approximately 10-15% of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinomas and represents one of the most genomically altered subtypes. It is associated with an aggressive biological phenotype and highlights the importance of effective systemic therapy.

NO actionable targeted therapy is currently available for this genomic profile based on currently approved drugs and validated biomarker-drug pairings. The KRAS mutation is NOT the G12C variant; sotorasib and adagrasib do not apply. The tumor is MSS and TMB-low, precluding pembrolizumab on these grounds. There is no HRD or BRCA/ATM deficiency to support PARP inhibitor use. There are no NTRK, ALK, RET, or ROS1 fusions.

Standard-of-care cytotoxic chemotherapy remains the backbone of therapy. FOLFIRINOX (oxaliplatin, irinotecan, leucovorin, 5-fluorouracil) and gemcitabine + nab-paclitaxel (Abraxane) are the preferred first-line regimens in PDAC with adequate performance status (ECOG 0-1). The SMAD4 deletion in this patient warrants attention in multidisciplinary discussion of local versus systemic therapy strategy.

Germline genetic testing is strongly recommended per NCCN guidelines.

HEPATOPANCREATOBILIARY ONCOLOGY — MULTIDISCIPLINARY TUMOR BOARD CONSULT NOTE

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: Hepatopancreatobiliary Medical Oncology
SERVICE: Gastrointestinal Oncology / HPB Tumor Board
NOTE TYPE: New Patient Consultation / Multidisciplinary Tumor Board Assessment

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 66-year-old retired pipe fitter presenting for multidisciplinary tumor board evaluation and oncologic consultation for newly diagnosed borderline resectable pancreatic head adenocarcinoma. He was referred following completion of staging workup including endoscopic ultrasound with FNA, pancreatic protocol CT, PET/CT, and molecular profiling.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mr. Kowalski came to medical attention approximately 3–4 weeks ago with a several-week history of progressive jaundice, epigastric discomfort radiating to the mid-back, and unintentional weight loss estimated at 5–6 kg over the preceding 2 months. He also reported new-onset greasy, light-colored stools and mild pruritus, consistent with obstructive biliary physiology. He denied hematemesis, melena, or hematochezia. Review of prior records does not identify antecedent abdominal imaging that would have captured an earlier lesion.

Initial laboratory evaluation revealed a total bilirubin of 2.1 mg/dL, direct bilirubin elevated, and serum CA 19-9 markedly elevated at 1200 U/mL. CEA was mildly elevated at 4.8 ng/mL. Liver enzymes were mildly elevated with a cholestatic pattern. An outpatient abdominal ultrasound prompted cross-sectional imaging, which revealed a 3.8 cm hypovascular mass in the head of the pancreas with upstream main pancreatic duct dilation to 6 mm and mild common bile duct dilation.

The patient subsequently underwent pancreatic protocol CT (triphasic CT abdomen/pelvis), which demonstrated the 3.8 cm ill-defined hypovascular mass in the pancreatic head with abutment of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) over approximately 120–150 degrees of circumference and short-segment abutment/encasement of the SMV-portal vein confluence without occlusion. Two periportal lymph nodes measuring up to 1.2 cm were noted, suspicious for nodal involvement. There was no hepatic or peritoneal metastatic disease.

Whole-body FDG PET/CT confirmed a hypermetabolic primary pancreatic head mass (SUVmax 8.4) with mildly FDG-avid periportal lymph nodes (SUVmax 3.1 and 2.8). No distant metastatic disease was identified.

Endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) with fine needle aspiration of the pancreatic head mass was performed and yielded diagnostic material. Cytopathology confirmed adenocarcinoma consistent with pancreatic ductal origin, with malignant glandular cells showing nuclear pleomorphism, irregular nuclear membranes, and prominent nucleoli. Immunohistochemistry on the cell block demonstrated CK7 positive, CK20 negative, CDX2 negative, MUC1 positive, MUC2 negative — a profile consistent with pancreaticobiliary origin. Diagnosis: MALIGNANT — ductal adenocarcinoma of the pancreas.

Next-generation sequencing (NGS) was performed on the EUS-FNA specimen. Relevant findings include: KRAS p.G12V mutation (VAF 42%), TP53 p.R248W mutation (VAF 38%), SMAD4 homozygous deletion, and CDKN2A homozygous deletion. Tumor mutational burden was 3.2 mutations/Mb (low). Microsatellite status: microsatellite stable (MSS). No homologous recombination deficiency detected (BRCA1, BRCA2, ATM, PALB2 all wild-type). No actionable fusions detected. PD-L1 CPS 1 (low). In summary, this is a KRAS G12V-driven, MSS, HRD-negative pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma with no currently actionable targeted therapy beyond standard cytotoxic chemotherapy.

Given biliary obstruction with a total bilirubin of 2.1 mg/dL, the patient underwent ERCP with placement of a plastic biliary stent, which was performed without complication. Bilirubin has trended toward improvement post-stenting. The case is now being presented to the HPB multidisciplinary tumor board for treatment planning.

Overall disease staging: Stage III, cT3N1M0 per AJCC 8th edition. Per NCCN/AHPBA criteria, the tumor is classified as BORDERLINE RESECTABLE based on SMA abutment < 180 degrees and short-segment SMV/PV involvement.

Positive: Progressive jaundice (improving post-biliary stenting), back pain (mid-epigastric radiating to mid-back), weight loss (~5–6 kg over 2 months), decreased appetite, early satiety, fatigue, light-colored/greasy stools (steatorrhea), mild pruritus (resolving).
Negative: No fever or chills. No hematemesis. No melena or hematochezia. No nausea or vomiting at rest. No new neurological symptoms. No chest pain or shortness of breath. No lower extremity edema. No dysuria.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
General: Alert and oriented x3. Mildly cachectic-appearing male in no acute distress. Mild residual scleral icterus, improved compared to pre-stenting reports.
Vitals: Blood pressure 132/78 mmHg, Heart rate 74 bpm, Temperature 37.0°C, Respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air. Weight 79 kg (BMI 26.2). Weight 5–6 kg below reported usual weight.
HEENT: Mild residual scleral icterus bilaterally. No lymphadenopathy in the cervical or supraclavicular chains. No Virchow's node palpated.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, rales, or rhonchi.
Abdomen: Soft. Mild epigastric tenderness to deep palpation. No guarding or rigidity. No palpable abdominal mass. No hepatomegaly. No splenomegaly. No shifting dullness. Bowel sounds normoactive. No Sister Mary Joseph nodule palpated.
Extremities: No peripheral edema. No calf tenderness. No skin changes.
Skin: Mild residual jaundice. No spider angiomata.
Neurological: Alert, oriented, appropriate. Cranial nerves II-XII intact. No gross motor or sensory deficits. Gait normal.
Lymph nodes: No palpable peripheral lymphadenopathy.
Performance Status: ECOG performance status 1 (restricted in physically strenuous activity but ambulatory and able to carry out light work).

---

IMAGING REVIEWED:

1. CT Abdomen/Pelvis with Pancreatic Protocol (triphasic): 3.8 cm ill-defined hypovascular pancreatic head mass with upstream main pancreatic duct dilation (6 mm) and mild common bile duct dilation. SMA abutment estimated at 120–150 degrees. Short-segment SMV/portal vein confluence involvement without occlusion. Two periportal lymph nodes up to 1.2 cm, suspicious for malignant involvement. No hepatic metastases. No peritoneal disease. No ascites.

2. FDG PET/CT Whole Body: Hypermetabolic pancreatic head primary (SUVmax 8.4). Mildly FDG-avid periportal nodes (SUVmax 3.1, 2.8). No distant metastatic disease. No peritoneal carcinomatosis confirmed.

3. EUS: Hypoechoic 3.8 cm mass in pancreatic head with ductal involvement. Vascular involvement consistent with CT findings. EUS-FNA performed.

---

PATHOLOGY / MOLECULAR REVIEW:
- EUS-FNA Cytology: Adenocarcinoma, consistent with pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. CK7+, CK20−, CDX2−, MUC1+.
- NGS: KRAS G12V (VAF 42%), TP53 R248W (VAF 38%), SMAD4 homozygous deletion, CDKN2A homozygous deletion. TMB low (3.2 mut/Mb). MSS. HRD-negative. No BRCA1/2, ATM, PALB2 mutations. No actionable fusions. PD-L1 CPS 1.

---

ASSESSMENT:

Mr. Kowalski is a 66-year-old male with newly diagnosed Stage III borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the head of the pancreas, cT3N1M0 (AJCC 8th edition). The tumor is 3.8 cm with SMA abutment of approximately 120–150 degrees and short-segment SMV/portal vein involvement, meeting NCCN criteria for borderline resectability. CA 19-9 is markedly elevated at 1200 U/mL. Molecular profile reveals KRAS G12V, TP53 R248W, SMAD4 deletion, and CDKN2A deletion — a high-risk molecular signature with no currently actionable targeted therapy (specifically, not a KRAS G12C mutation and therefore not eligible for sotorasib or adagrasib). MSS status and low TMB preclude benefit from immune checkpoint inhibitors. BRCA2/ATM wild-type status indicates no PARP inhibitor indication.

Biliary decompression has been performed via ERCP plastic stent. The patient has ECOG PS 1 with adequate organ function and performance to tolerate aggressive cytotoxic regimens.

The case was presented in full detail at the HPB multidisciplinary tumor board today. In attendance were HPB surgery, radiation oncology, medical oncology, interventional radiology, gastroenterology, diagnostic radiology, and pathology.

---

MULTIDISCIPLINARY TUMOR BOARD DISCUSSION SUMMARY:

The board reviewed all available imaging (CT pancreatic protocol, PET/CT), pathology (EUS-FNA cytology), molecular profiling (NGS), and relevant laboratory data. There was consensus that primary upfront surgical resection is not recommended given the degree of vascular involvement — specifically SMA abutment and SMV/portal vein contact — which would carry a high risk for positive margins (R1 or R2 resection). The surgical team concurs that primary resection at this time would be technically challenging and carries significant risk of incomplete resection.

The board consensus recommendation is neoadjuvant chemotherapy with the goal of downsizing the tumor, improving the vascular relationships, and converting this patient to a state more favorable for surgical resection with clear margins. Based on the published evidence from the PRODIGE 24/CCTG PA.6 trial demonstrating superiority of modified FOLFIRINOX over gemcitabine in metastatic disease, and extrapolation of this to the neoadjuvant setting supported by multiple institutional series and the Alliance A021501 trial experience, the board recommends neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX chemotherapy.

Plan: FOLFIRINOX for 8 cycles (approximately 4 months), with restaging CT pancreatic protocol after cycle 4 and after cycle 8, and reassessment of resectability at completion of chemotherapy. If good response with improved vascular anatomy, the plan will be to consider consolidative chemoradiation followed by reassessment for Whipple procedure.

Germline genetic testing: BRCA1/2 and ATM germline testing ordered today per NCCN guidelines for all patients with pancreatic adenocarcinoma, regardless of family history. Results pending.

Regarding diabetes: Metformin to be held on chemotherapy infusion days (days 1–2 of each cycle) given IV contrast use and risk of lactic acidosis; otherwise to be continued. Glycemic management to be optimized throughout treatment.

---

PLAN:

1. NEOADJUVANT CHEMOTHERAPY — FOLFIRINOX:
   - Oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2 IV over 2 hours, Day 1
   - Irinotecan 180 mg/m2 IV over 90 minutes, Day 1
   - Leucovorin (folinic acid) 400 mg/m2 IV over 2 hours, Day 1 (concurrent with irinotecan)
   - 5-Fluorouracil 400 mg/m2 IV bolus, then 2400 mg/m2 IV over 46 hours continuous infusion
   - Repeat every 14 days (q2w); target 8 cycles total
   - GCSF support: Pegfilgrastim (Neulasta) 6 mg subcutaneously on Day 2 of each cycle for primary prophylaxis (per institutional protocol for patients at high risk of febrile neutropenia given age and FOLFIRINOX-associated myelosuppression)
   - Antiemetic prophylaxis: Ondansetron 8 mg IV plus dexamethasone 12 mg IV pre-chemotherapy on Day 1; prochlorperazine 10 mg PO q6h PRN breakthrough nausea
   - Irinotecan-related cholinergic prophylaxis: Atropine 0.25–0.5 mg IV/SQ for acute cholinergic symptoms; loperamide 4 mg PO at first loose stool, then 2 mg q2h during waking hours for delayed diarrhea (max 16 mg/day)
   - Metformin: hold on chemotherapy days (Day 1 and Day 2) as noted above; restart Day 3
   - Dose reduction guidelines: planned dose reductions per institutional protocol for grade 3–4 hematologic or non-hematologic toxicities

2. BILIARY STENT MANAGEMENT:
   - Plastic biliary stent in place; gastroenterology to schedule stent exchange in approximately 8 weeks to prevent stent occlusion
   - Monitor bilirubin monthly; hepatobiliary panel at each visit

3. RESTAGING:
   - CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol after cycle 4 (approximately 2 months)
   - CA 19-9 before each cycle
   - Repeat comprehensive restaging CT after cycle 8, followed by tumor board reassessment for resectability

4. GERMLINE TESTING:
   - BRCA1, BRCA2, ATM (and PALB2) germline testing: blood draw ordered today; genetic counseling referral placed
   - Results expected within 3–4 weeks

5. NUTRITIONAL SUPPORT:
   - Dietitian referral placed — significant weight loss (5–6 kg) and likely evolving pancreatic exocrine insufficiency
   - Consider initiation of pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT, e.g., Creon) if steatorrhea persists or worsens; will reassess at first follow-up
   - High-calorie, high-protein oral supplements recommended

6. DIABETES MANAGEMENT:
   - Continue metformin with modification per chemotherapy days as above
   - HbA1c 7.8%; endocrinology co-management to be considered if glycemic control worsens during chemotherapy

7. FOLLOW-UP:
   - First chemotherapy cycle to be initiated within the next 5–7 days pending patient readiness and port placement
   - Port-a-cath placement scheduled through interventional radiology (required for 46-hour 5-FU infusion)
   - Return to medical oncology clinic on Day 14 (prior to cycle 2) for toxicity assessment, labs (CBC, CMP, CA 19-9)

8. PATIENT EDUCATION:
   - Extensive counseling provided to patient and wife regarding diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment plan
   - Goals of neoadjuvant therapy discussed: to shrink the tumor, improve the relationship with blood vessels, and achieve a situation where complete surgical removal may be possible — with the ultimate goal of resection and potential for cure or prolonged disease-free survival
   - Risks and expected side effects of FOLFIRINOX discussed in detail including: neuropathy, myelosuppression (neutropenia), fatigue, nausea/vomiting, diarrhea, alopecia, mucositis
   - Patient and his wife verbalized understanding. Patient expressed desire to pursue all appropriate treatment options and is agreeable to the neoadjuvant plan.

Baseline CA 19-9: 1200 U/mL (to be used as marker of treatment response).
ECOG PS: 1.

CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH IV CONTRAST — PANCREATIC PROTOCOL (TRIPHASIC)

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Raymond Kowalski, 66-year-old male with biopsy-proven borderline resectable pancreatic head ductal adenocarcinoma (cT3N1M0, Stage III). Currently undergoing neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX chemotherapy; this examination is performed for mid-treatment restaging after completion of cycle 4 (approximately 8 weeks of therapy). Baseline CA 19-9 was 1200 U/mL; most recent CA 19-9 reported at 380 U/mL. Comparison: Prior pancreatic protocol CT performed at initial staging.

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed in the non-contrast, arterial (pancreatic parenchymal), portal venous, and delayed phases following administration of 100 mL of nonionic intravenous contrast (iohexol 350 mg I/mL) at a rate of 4 mL/second. Oral contrast was not administered. Axial, coronal, and sagittal reconstructions reviewed with dedicated pancreatic windowing. Curvilinear and maximum intensity projection reconstructions were reviewed for vascular assessment.

---

FINDINGS:

PANCREAS:
There is a persistent ill-defined, hypoenhancing mass in the head and uncinate process of the pancreas. On the current examination, the mass measures approximately 3.1 cm in greatest axial dimension (previously 3.8 cm on the baseline staging CT), representing a meaningful decrease in overall size. The margins of the mass remain irregular and poorly circumscribed, blending with the surrounding desmoplastic stroma, though there appears to be a subtle decrease in the degree of perilesional soft tissue stranding and associated mesenteric haziness in the immediate vicinity of the mass when compared to the baseline examination. A small central area of low attenuation within the lesion may represent early necrosis or treatment-related change, though this finding is nonspecific. The degree of upstream main pancreatic ductal dilatation appears marginally decreased, now measuring approximately 5 mm in maximal diameter (previously 6 mm), suggesting partial relief of ductal obstruction. A biliary stent is in place within the distal common bile duct; the upstream intrahepatic bile ducts and common hepatic duct appear decompressed and within normal caliber, consistent with adequate biliary drainage.

VASCULAR RELATIONSHIPS:
On the prior examination, the pancreatic head mass was noted to abut the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) over approximately 120–150 degrees of its circumference. On the current study, the degree of soft tissue contact along the right lateral and posterior aspect of the SMA appears reduced. The fat plane between the leading edge of the mass and the SMA is still not completely restored, but the extent of circumferential soft tissue contact now appears to involve less than 90 degrees of the arterial wall. No teardrop deformity or luminal irregularity of the SMA is identified. There is no evidence of arterial encasement.

The superior mesenteric vein–portal vein (SMV-PV) confluence continues to demonstrate short-segment contact with the mass along its posterior aspect. The degree of this contact appears roughly stable compared to the baseline study. The venous lumen remains patent without evidence of occlusion, thrombosis, or definitive encasement. No teardrop deformity of the SMV is appreciated. The confluence appears widely patent on the portal venous phase images.

The celiac axis and its major branches (splenic artery, common hepatic artery, left gastric artery) are patent and without evidence of involvement by tumor. The hepatic artery proper, right hepatic artery, and left hepatic artery arise in typical fashion and appear uninvolved.

LYMPH NODES:
Two previously identified periportal/hepatoduodenal ligament lymph nodes appear decreased in overall size. The largest node in this region now measures approximately 0.8 cm in short-axis dimension (previously up to 1.2 cm). The nodes are no longer as conspicuous and appear borderline in size. No new pathologically enlarged lymph nodes are identified in the celiac, peripancreatic, retroperitoneal, or mesenteric distributions.

LIVER:
The liver is normal in size and demonstrates homogeneous enhancement in all phases. No focal hepatic lesions are identified. No hypervascular or hypovascular hepatic masses. No biliary ductal dilatation. Portal vein is patent. Hepatic veins and inferior vena cava are patent.

GALLBLADDER AND BILIARY SYSTEM:
A previously placed plastic biliary stent is again visualized traversing the distal common bile duct. The gallbladder is present and unremarkable without wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, or gallstones. The intrahepatic ducts appear decompressed.

SPLEEN:
The spleen is normal in size and demonstrates homogeneous enhancement without focal lesions. No splenomegaly. Splenic vein is patent.

ADRENAL GLANDS:
Bilateral adrenal glands appear within normal limits with no focal nodules or masses beyond expected adrenal limb width.

KIDNEYS AND URETERS:
Bilateral kidneys are symmetric in size and demonstrate normal cortical thickness and enhancement. No renal masses, hydronephrosis, or nephrolithiasis. No ureteral abnormality.

STOMACH AND SMALL BOWEL:
The stomach is unremarkable. The duodenum is partially obscured by the adjacent pancreatic head mass, as expected; no upstream gastric or duodenal obstruction is appreciated. No small bowel obstruction or abnormal mural thickening. No mesenteric edema.

COLON:
The large bowel is not distended and shows no evidence of wall thickening or mass. Appendix is visualized and within normal limits.

PERITONEUM AND MESENTERY:
No peritoneal nodules, omental thickening, or soft tissue implants to suggest peritoneal carcinomatosis. No free fluid. No ascites. Mesenteric fat stranding is mild and appears decreased compared to the prior examination, likely related to treatment response.

RETROPERITONEUM:
The aorta is mildly ectatic in the infrarenal position (2.4 cm), unchanged and not aneurysmal. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. No retroperitoneal mass or hematoma.

BONY STRUCTURES AND SOFT TISSUES:
The visualized osseous structures are intact without lytic or blastic lesions to suggest osseous metastases. Mild multilevel degenerative changes are noted in the lumbar spine. Abdominal wall is intact without hernia or mass. Subcutaneous soft tissues are unremarkable.

PELVIS:
Bladder is well distended and unremarkable. Prostate gland is mildly enlarged, consistent with benign prostatic hypertrophy. No pelvic free fluid. No pelvic lymphadenopathy. Iliac vessels are patent.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. PANCREATIC HEAD MASS — DECREASED IN SIZE: The hypoenhancing mass in the pancreatic head and uncinate process has decreased from 3.8 cm to approximately 3.1 cm since the baseline staging examination, consistent with a meaningful morphologic response to neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX chemotherapy. Accompanying reduction in perilesional soft tissue stranding and slight improvement in upstream pancreatic ductal dilatation further support treatment effect.

2. IMPROVED VASCULAR ABUTMENT: The degree of soft tissue contact between the primary mass and the superior mesenteric artery appears diminished, now estimated at less than 90 degrees of circumference compared to 120–150 degrees at baseline. No arterial encasement. The SMA fat plane shows early signs of partial reconstitution, though complete clearance has not been achieved.

3. SMV-PV INTERFACE — STABLE WITHOUT OCCLUSION: Short-segment contact of the tumor with the SMV-portal vein confluence is again noted, appearing stable in extent without new venous compromise. The venous confluence remains widely patent.

4. INTERVAL DECREASE IN PERIPORTAL LYMPH NODES: The previously suspicious periportal lymph nodes have decreased in size, now measuring up to 0.8 cm (previously 1.2 cm). These nodes remain borderline and warrant continued monitoring, but the trend is encouraging.

5. NO DISTANT METASTATIC DISEASE: No hepatic lesions, no peritoneal implants, no ascites, and no new lymphadenopathy suspicious for distant spread. No evidence of hepatic or pulmonary metastases on the current study.

6. BILIARY STENT IN PLACE WITH ADEQUATE DECOMPRESSION: The distal common bile duct stent remains appropriately positioned with decompressed biliary system.

Overall, the imaging findings are consistent with a favorable response to neoadjuvant chemotherapy with a reduction in primary tumor bulk, improved vascular anatomy relative to the SMA, and decrease in regional lymph node conspicuity. The constellation of findings suggests the tumor may be trending toward a more favorable surgical posture, though continued neoadjuvant therapy and repeat restaging after additional cycles would be appropriate to further characterize the degree of response and reassess resectability.

scenario_blurb: Locally advanced pancreatic adenocarcinoma (borderline resectable) in a 66-year-old with tumor abutting the SMA and SMV. CA 19-9 elevated to 1200. Treated with neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX for 8 cycles with CA 19-9 decline to 85 and partial response on imaging. Then chemoradiation with capecitabine. Successfully undergoes Whipple procedure with negative margins. Adjuvant gemcitabine/capecitabine complicated by pancreatic fistula.
